narrative: Dear Alex, imagine the admissions team looking at your application not just as a stack of numbers, but as the opening chapters of your story as a future lawyer—and right now, those chapters have painted a picture that worries them about your chances of passing the bar exam. Your Year 3 grades placed you in the 2nd decile at your school, which stood out as the biggest red flag, suggesting that even after building some foundation, you were still struggling to rise among your peers. That concern echoes back to your 1st-year decile of 3rd place, reinforcing a pattern of academic hurdles early on that makes it hard for the model to see you pulling ahead when it counts most. Your LSAT score of 35.5, while respectable, didn't quite shine bright enough to offset that, pulling the prediction further toward the risk of failure. With your family in the middle-income bracket, the model sees added pressures that often make the intense path to the bar tougher without extra cushions. As a white male going full-time, those traits fit profiles where the data shows slimmer margins for success in this high-stakes arena, nudged along by your undergraduate GPA of 3.2, which hints at consistency but not the standout excellence needed. Together, these pieces wove a narrative of about 50.5% odds of not passing the bar, tipping the scales against your law school spot. But here's the hopeful turn in your story: this isn't the end of the book. You've got time to rewrite those early chapters—maybe by targeting retakes, bolstering study habits, or seeking mentorship that lifts students like you higher. The model isn't judging your potential; it's just spotting patterns from thousands of journeys like yours. What small step could you take today to shift that decile upward and flip this prediction? Your full story is still yours to author.
